content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 20, 2016

Rob Williams, AIA
Principal
Studio 6 Architects, Inc.
2753 Camino Capistrano, Suite B2
San Clemente, CA 92672

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-240

Dear Mr. Williams:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented. Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as Section 1090 or common law conflict of interest.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, City of San Juan Capistrano Planning Commissioner Rob Williams, from taking part in the governmental decision on whether to recommend the approval of amendments to the Historic Town Center Master Plan (the "HTCMP")?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in the decision on whether to recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP.

FACTS

You are a Planning Commissioner for the City of San Juan Capistrano, a Principal at Studio 6 Architects, Inc. ("Studio 6"), and the owner of a fifty percent interest in Studio 6 valued in excess of $2,000. Currently, the HTCMP is inconsistent with the City's General Plan. More specifically, the HTCMP does not authorize residential zoning within the historic town center and the General Plan does and the HTCMP's floor-area ratio, height, and setback requirements for those buildings

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-240
Page No. 2

deviate from the General Plan's requirements for those buildings. The Planning Commission will
soon consider whether to recommend approval of amendments to the HTCMP that would resolve
these inconsistencies with the General Plan.

Friess Development Group was the general contractor on a recent remodel of the Egan
House, a historic building within the historic town center owned by William Griffith. Studio 6 was
a subcontractor and the architect of record for the interior demolition. You earned in excess of $500
over the past 12 months from Studio 6 for your work on the project. Studio 6 has completed its
work on the project, and Studio 6's contract with Friess Development Group has been fully
performed. You advised that the Egan House would not require any additional work as a result of
the approval of the amendments to the HTCMP if the amendments are adopted.

In the recent past there was a controversy among concerned residents about a development
project to build a hotel within the City's historic town center. You were a member of a group of
concerned residents that filed a lawsuit against the City seeking to enjoin the hotel project. The
lawsuit argued that the City Council should not have approved the project because it was
inconsistent with the General Plan's floor-area ratio, height, and setback requirements for buildings
within the historic town center. Before the lawsuit was adjudicated, the plans for the project fell
through, and that property owner is no longer pursuing that hotel project. Although the lawsuit is
still pending, you and the other plaintiffs are not pursuing a money judgment or attorney's fees from
the City.

You unsuccessfully ran for the City Council in 2014. Mr. Griffith, the owner of the Egan
House, and his wife and two children each contributed $500 to your campaign. Mr. Griffith now
plans to build a hotel on land he purchased last year east of El Camino Real from Mission San Juan
Capistrano.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a
conflict of interest may arise under the Act. Of those interests, your interests implicated by the
decision on whether to recommend approval of the HTCMP and the facts presented are as follows:

• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000
or more. (Section 87103(a).)

• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status,
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)

File No. A-15-240
Page No. 3

• Your personal finances, including those of your immediate family. (Section 87103.)

You have an interest in Studio 6 as a business entity because you have a 50-percent ownership interest in the firm worth more than $2,000 (Section 87103(a)) and because you are a Principal with the firm (Section 87103(d)). You also have an interest in Studio 6 as a source of income because you have earned in excess of $500 from the firm in the 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) Under Section 82030(a), if a public official owns a 10-percent or greater interest in a business, a source of income to that business is also a source of income to the official. You own more than 10-percent of Studio 6, and Studio 6 has earned more than $500 from Friess Development Group in the 12 months prior to the decision. Therefore, you also have an interest in Friess Development Group as a source of income.

At issue is whether you also have an interest in Mr. Griffith, the owner of the Egan House, as a source of income. An official has an interest in an individual as a source of income if the individual "has the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies" of a business entity in which the official has an interest as a source of income. (Regulation 18700.1(a)(2)(B).) Based on the facts presented, there is no indication that Mr. Griffith has the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of Friess Development Group or Studio 6. Furthermore, the Commission generally advises that a contractor is the sole source of income to subcontractors, even though subcontractors are normally paid from funds collected from the contractor's client. (See Sauer Advice Letter. No. A-95-373.) Therefore, we find that you do not have an interest in Mr. Griffith as a source of income.

Thus, with respect to the decision at hand, you have interests in Studio 6, Friess Development Group, and your personal finances.

Foreseeability:

In order to determine if you have a conflict of interest under the Act with respect to the decision on whether to recommend approval of amendments to the HTCMP, we must consider whether a financial effect of the decision on one or more of your interests would be reasonably foreseeable. Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) In this case, your interests are not explicitly involved in the decision on whether to recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP. Therefore, an effect of the decision on one or more of your interests is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

The Planning Commission's decision on whether to recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP could affect the floor-area ratio, height, and setback requirements applicable to buildings, and whether residential zoning is allowed, within the City's historic town center. Thus, the approval of the amendments to the HTCMP may result in increased demand for architects, such as Studio 6, and contractors, such as Friess Development Group, that work within the historic town center. However, to the extent that you have not been approached by any client or prospective client regarding a project dependent on the amendments in question, any attempt to gauge the extent of the increased demand for these services would be speculative, as would any

File No. A-15-240
Page No. 4

attempt to attribute that demand between competing architects and contractors. Furthermore, you
advised that the Egan House would not require any additional work, and have not identified any
other client of Studio 6 or Friess Development Group that would require additional work, as a result
of the City Council's approval of the amendments. Therefore, the potential for Studio 6 or Friess
Development Group to acquire additional work as a result of the Planning Commission decision is
hypothetical or theoretical.³

Because the potential for Studio 6 to acquire additional work as a result of the Planning
Commission decision is hypothetical or theoretical, and because you have not provided facts
supporting any other particular effect on your personal finances, any financial effect of the decision
on your personal finances is also hypothetical or theoretical. Aside from the hypothetical or
theoretical additional income to Studio 6 from the potential additional work resultant from the City
Council's potential approval of the amendments, the facts do not indicate that the Planning
Commission's decision will affect your personal finances in any other way.³

Accordingly, we find that the financial effects of the Planning Commission's decision on
whether to recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP on Studio 6, Friess
Development Group, or your personal finances, if any, are hypothetical or theoretical and,
therefore, not reasonably foreseeable.

Section 84308:

Campaign contributions generally do not give rise to a conflict of interest for elected
officials under the Act, except in certain narrow situations where an official is a member of an
appointed board or commission and is also running for office and receiving campaign contributions.
(Section 84308.) On numerous occasions, we have advised that the receipt of campaign
contributions does not give rise to a conflict of interest because the Act expressly exempts
campaign contributions from the definitions of "income" and "gift." (Section 82028, 82030; See
Dauber Advice Letter, No. A-15-151; Gwinn Advice Letter, No. I-03-217; Rudansky Advice Letter,
No. A-03-113; Larocque Advice Letter, No. I-99-161.)

As applicable to the facts presented, subdivision (c) of Section 84308 requires an officer of
an agency who received a contribution of more than $250 within the preceding 12 months from a
party or from any participant shall disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding prior to
rendering any decision in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use
pending before the agency. That subdivision also prohibits an officer of an agency from making,
participating in the making, or attempting to use his or her official position to influence the decision
in a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use pending before the agency

³ If you have been approached by a specific client regarding a project dependent on the amendments, the
conclusions contained herein do not apply and you should seek additional assistance and provide the relevant facts

³ The law suit to enjoin the City's approval of the now defunct hotel project within the historic town center is
not a consideration in our analysis of your interest in your personal finances. You and the other plaintiffs in that lawsuit
were only pursuing an injunction against that project, and not money damages or attorney fees from the City. Also, that
lawsuit was not directly related to, and would not be decided pursuant to, the Planning Commission's decision on
whether to recommend the approval of the amendments.

File No. A-15-240
Page No. 5

if the officer has willfully or knowingly received a contribution of more than $250 within the
preceding 12 months from a party or a participant. (Section 84308(c).)

Your facts indicate that Mr. Griffith and his wife and two children each contributed $500 to
your unsuccessful campaign to become a City Councilmember in 2014. The Planning
Commission's decision on whether to recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP is
scheduled to take place in the near future, well beyond 12 months since Mr. Griffith and his wife
and children made contributions to your City Council Campaign. Therefore, you are not subject to
Section 84308's disclosure requirement and prohibition described above with respect to the
Planning Commission's decision.

Because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the Planning Commission's
decision on whether to recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP on your financial
interests, and because the prohibition contained in subdivision (c) of Section 84308 is not applicable
to you with respect to the Planning Commission's decision, the Act does not prohibit you from
taking part in that decision.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental 
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one 
or more of the public official's interests. Section 87103 also sets forth the interests from which a 
conflict of interest may arise under the Act. Of those interests, your interests implicated by the 
decision on whether to recommend approval of the HTC MP and the facts presented are as follows: 
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect im·estment worth S2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).) 
• Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in the 
regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating SSOO or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)  
• Your personal finances, including those of your immediate family. (Section 87103.) 
You have an interest in Studio 6 as a business entity because you ha,·e a SO-percent 
ownership interest in the firm worth more than S2,000 (Section 87103(a)) and because you are a 
Principal with the firm (Section 87103{d)). You also have an interest in Studio 6 as a source of 
income because you have earned in excess of SSOO from the firm in the 12 months prior to the 
decision. (Section 87103(c).) Under Section 82030(a). if a public official owns a IO-percent or 
greater interest in a business, a source of income to that business is also a source of income to the 
official. You own more than 10-percent of Studio 6, and Studio 6 has earned more than of SSOO 
from Friess Development Group in the 12 months prior to the decision. Therefore, you also have an 
interest in Friess Development Group as a source of income. 
At issue is whether you also ha,·e an interest in Mr. Griffith, the owner of the Egan House, 
as a source of income. An official has an interest in an individual as a source of income if the 
individual "has the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies" of a 
business entity in which the official has an interest as a source of income. (Regulation 
18700.1 (a)(2)(B).) Based on the facts presented, there is no indication that Mr. Griffith has the 
power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of Friess Development Group

income to subcontractors, even though subcontractors arc normally paid from funds collected from 
the contractor's client. (Sec Sauer Advice Letter, No. A-95-373.) Therefore, we find that you do not 
have an interest in Mr. Griffith as a source of income. 
Thus, with respect to the decision at hand, you have interests in Studio 6, Friess 
Development Group, and your personal finances. 
Foreseeability: 
In order to determine if you have a conflict of interest under the Act with respect to the 
decision on whether to recommend approval of amendments to the HTC MP, we must consider 
whether a financial effect of the decision on one or more of your interests would be reasonably 
foreseeable. Under the Act. an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is 
explicitly involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved'' if the interest is a named 
party in, or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701 (a).) In this case, your interests are not 
explicitly involved in the decision on whether to recommend appro\·al of the amendments to the 
HTCMP. Therefore, an effect of the decision on one or more of your interests is reasonably 
foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. (Regulation l870l(b).) 
The Planning Commission's decision on whether to recommend approval of the 
amendments to the HTCMP could affect the floor-area ratio, height, and setback requirements 
applicable to buildings, and whether residential zoning is allowed, within the City's historic town 
center. Thus, the approval of the amendments to the HTCMP may result in increased demand for 
architects, such as Studio 6, and contractors, such as Friess Development Group, that work within 
the historic town center. However, to the extent that you have not been approached by any client or 
prospective client regarding a project dependent on the amendments in question, any attempt to 
gauge the extent of the increased demand for these services would be speculative, as would any  
attempt to attribute that demand between competing architects and contractors. Furthermore, you 
advised that the Egan House would not require any additional work, and have not identified any 
other client of Studio 6 or Friess Development Group that would require additional work, as a result 
of the City Council's approval of the amendments. Therefore, the potential for Studio 6 or Friess 
Development Group to acquire additional work as a result of the Planning Commission decision is 
hypothetical or theoreticat.2 
Because the potential for Studio 6 to acquire additional work as a result of the Planning 
Commission decision is hypothetical or theoretical, and because you have not provided facts 
supporting any other particular effect on your personal finances, any financial effect of the decision 
on your personal finances is also hypothetical or theoretical. Aside from the hypothetical or 
theoretical additional income to Studio 6 from the potential additional work resultant from the City 
Council's potential approval of the amendments, the facts do not indicate that the Planning 
Commission's decision will affect your personal finances in any other way.3 
Accordingly, we find that the financial effects of the Planning Commission's decision on

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you, City of San Juan Capistrano Planning Commissioner Rob 
Williams, from taking part in the go\'emmental decision on whether to recommend the approval of 
amendments to the Historic Town Center Master Plan (the "HTCMP")?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in the decision on whether to 
recommend approval of the amendments to the HTCMP.
